title: Devilled mackerel
subtitle: Grilled mackerel fillets with a hot, sharp mustard glaze
excerpt: Fresh mackerel fillets coated in a sharp, spiced devilled butter with mustard and cayenne, then grilled until the skin blisters. The combination of oily fish and pungent spicing is one of the best in the British repertoire.
sourceNotes: Devilled fish, including mackerel, is documented in British cookery from the Victorian period. Isabella Beeton includes devilled fish preparations in Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. The technique of coating oily fish in a mustard-and-cayenne devilled butter and grilling is characteristic of nineteenth-century British cookery.

content.text: Devilled mackerel is one of the most direct pleasures in British fish cookery: a rich, oily fish that stands up to powerful flavouring, with a skin that blisters under the grill and a devilled butter that melts into the flesh. The combination of mustard and cayenne against the fat of the mackerel is exactly the right balance of punchy and rich.

Mackerel must be very fresh — ideally bought on the day. Stale mackerel smells strongly and the texture is soft and unpleasant. A fresh mackerel has bright eyes, red gills, and stiff flesh. If in doubt, ask your fishmonger when it arrived. The fish should cook skin-side up under a very hot grill.
Make the devilled butter and grill

Preheat the grill to its highest setting. Mix {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter with {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, {{wholegrain-mustard}} of wholegrain mustard, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, cayenne pepper, and {{lemon-juice}} of lemon juice. Season with salt.

Line a grill tray with foil and lay the {{mackerel-fillets}} mackerel fillets on it skin-side up. Spread the devilled butter generously over the skin. Grill 8 to 10 cm from the heat for 6 to 8 minutes until the skin is blistered and charred in places and the fish is cooked through. The flesh should flake easily when pressed. Scatter with chopped parsley and serve immediately with crusty bread and a green salad or watercress.

items.symptom: Skin is pale and not crisping
items.cause: Grill is not hot enough, or fish is too far from the heat
items.fix: The grill must be at full heat and the fish should be 8 to 10 cm from the element. Mackerel skin needs direct, intense heat to blister.

items.symptom: Mackerel smells very strong and the flesh is soft
items.fix: Fresh mackerel is essential — it deteriorates faster than most fish. If in doubt, buy a different fish for this recipe.

items.symptom: Devilled butter has burned but fish is still raw
items.cause: Grill too hot and fish too close to the element
items.fix: Move the fish further from the grill and watch carefully. The butter will colour quickly; the fish needs 6 to 8 minutes at moderate distance.
The devilled butter should taste bold: sharp, hot, and pungent. Taste it before spreading and increase the cayenne and mustard to suit.

content.text: Devilled preparations in British cooking, meaning dishes coated in mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce then grilled or fried, were most common in the Victorian and Edwardian periods, when they appeared at breakfast, as savouries at the end of dinner, and as supper dishes. Mackerel was a natural choice for the treatment: its rich, fatty flesh absorbs flavour readily and its strong taste is not overwhelmed by pungent seasoning. Oily fish and sharp spicing is a combination with precedent across many cuisines; the British version expressed it through the devilled butter, which is a practical and elegant technique.
